paper_title: STRIDE: Training Data Attribution via Sparse Recovery from Subset Perturbations

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Training Data Attribution (TDA) seeks to trace a model's predictions back to its training data. The gold standard for TDA relies on causal interventions, observing how a model changes when data is added or removed, but repeated retraining is computationally challenging for Large Language Models (LLMs). Consequently, most approaches approximate this effect in the parameter space using gradients. However, tracking gradients across billions of parameters is not only prohibitively expensive but relies on local approximations. In this work, we propose a shift: rather than estimating parameter changes, we model the functional effect of training data in the activation space. We introduce STRIDE (Steering-based Training Data Influence Decomposition), a framework that formulates TDA as a sparse recovery problem in the spirit of compressive sensing. STRIDE learns lightweight "steering operators" that mimic the behavioral shift caused by training on data subsets. By measuring how these operators perturb test predictions, we recover individual training example influences via sparse linear decomposition. STRIDE achieves state-of-the-art for LLM pre-training attribution while being an order of magnitude ($13\times$) faster than previous art. We further validate its practical utility through downstream applications including data selection, data contamination, and qualitative analysis.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Training Data Attribution (TDA) seeks to trace models predictions back to its training data. The gold standard for TDA relies on causal interventions, observing how model changes when data is added or removed, but repeated retraining is computationally challenging for Large Language Models (LLMs). Consequently, most approaches approximate this effect in the parameter space using gradients. However, tracking gradients across billions of parameters is not only prohibitively expensive but relies on local approximations. In this work, we propose shift: rather than estimating parameter changes, we model the functional effect of training data in the activation space. We introduce STRIDE (Steering-based Training Data Influence Decomposition), framework that formulates TDA as sparse recovery problem in the spirit of compressive sensing. STRIDE learns lightweight steering operators that mimic the behavioral shift caused by training on data subsets. By measuring how these operators perturb test predictions, we recover individual training example influences via sparse linear decomposition. On LLM pre-training attribution, STRIDE achieves the highest Linear Datamodeling Score (LDS), outperforming the strongest baselines while being over 12 faster. We further validate its practical utility through downstream applications, including data selection, data contamination, and qualitative analysis.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: The capabilities of modern Large Language Models (LLMs) are intrinsically tied to the massive corpora they are trained on [1, 2]. Yet, the pre-training process remains largely black box. When model exhibits specific behavior, retrieves fact, or makes an error, tracing that output back to its source data is challenging. Training Data Attribution (TDA) aims to solve this by quantifying the causal influence of individual training examples on specific model predictions [3, 4]. Reliable TDA enables auditing model behavior, detecting memorization [5], debugging harmful outputs [6], and curating higher-quality datasets for future training runs [7]. TDA is counterfactual problem: how would the models prediction change if specific example were omitted from the training set? The most rigorous way to answer this is through actual retraining, such as computing Leave-One-Out (LOO) influence or building extensive Data Models [3]. Since repeatedly retraining LLMs is computationally prohibitive; the dominant paradigm is to approximate the counterfactual using parameter-space gradients, most notably via Influence Functions [4, 8]. Preprint 2026. 2 is irrational. Attribution reveals it mimicked the structure in its response after Figure 1: Top: OLMo-2-7B generates structurally correct but algebraically flawed proof that 3 and 3 3 proofs in the training data. Bottom: When asked to justify an AI lying, Qwen-2.5-32B constructs privacy-defense rationalization. Attribution traces this framing to conjunction of journalism about sentient AI and policy text on data rights, showing how base models assemble moral frameworks from distinct pre-training narratives. However, applying gradient-based methods to LLMs introduces severe bottlenecks. Materializing and storing gradients for billions of parameters across many examples requires enormous memory and compute. While recent projection methods like EKFAC [9] and LOGRA [10] improve efficiency, they still operate in the massive parameter space. Furthermore, gradient methods rely heavily on the assumption that models loss landscape is locally convex and that the model has reached strict convergence; however, these assumptions are violated in deep learning [11, 12]. To bypass these computational barriers, an alternative line of work explores representation-based methods [13], which estimate influence using embedding similarities [14] or learned scoring functions [15]. While highly scalable, these approaches often rely on heuristic feature spaces that lack the rigorous causal grounding of actual retraining, and they particularly struggle to capture the complex dynamics of LLM pre-training. This motivates the central question of our work: Can we recover retraining-level training data attribution by modeling how data subsets shift models activations, rather than its parameters? We answer affirmatively and propose to bypass both the parameter-space bottlenecks and the heuristic limitations of representation methods. We hypothesize that the causal impact of training data can be more robustly and efficiently modeled as functional shifts in the models activation space, view supported by recent work showing that low-rank activation perturbations can faithfully replicate behavioral changes induced by fine-tuning [16, 17]. When an LLM is trained on subset of data, it induces systematic changes in its internal representations. Rather than calculating how weights should adjust, we can model how the activations should be steered to produce the updated predictions. Building on this insight, we introduce STRIDE (Steering-based Training Data Influence Decomposition), which formulates training data attribution as sparse recovery problem over activation-space perturbations. The framework operates in two stages. First, we learn lightweight, low-rank steering operators that are applied to the activations of frozen model. These operators are optimized to explicitly mimic the change in the models output probabilities when it is trained on specific, randomly sampled subsets of the training data. Second, at inference time, we apply these operators to test query to generate perturbation response vector. Since each steering operator corresponds to known subset of training examples, its response provides an aggregate measurement of the examples that influence the query. Since given prediction typically depends on small fraction of the training corpus, STRIDE recovers individual influences from these subset-level measurements using sparse recovery, in the spirit of compressive sensing [18]. 2 By operating in the low-dimensional activation space, STRIDE removes the need to compute, store, or invert massive parameter gradients. More importantly, because the steering operators are explicitly fitted to actual retraining subset responses, STRIDE maintains strong causal grounding that heuristic similarity methods lack. We evaluate STRIDE using the rigorous Linear Datamodeling Score (LDS) protocol [3]. Our results demonstrate that STRIDE achieves state-of-the-art attribution accuracy for the challenging task of LLM pre-training, outperforming existing gradient-based and representation-based baselines while being an order of magnitude more computationally efficient ( 5.2). We also demonstrate its robustness on standard instruction-tuning (SFT) benchmarks ( 5.3). Finally, we validate that the attributions produced by STRIDE are highly actionable by demonstrating strong performance on downstream applications ( 5.4, 5.5 and B). In summary, our main contributions are: 1. We introduce novel perspective for Training Data Attribution, moving away from parameterspace approximations to model the causal effect of training data as shifts in the activation space. 2. We propose STRIDE, highly efficient framework that learns activation-space steering operators to mimic subset retraining, and recovers per-example influence via compressive sensing. 3. We demonstrate that STRIDE achieves state-of-the-art attribution accuracy (measured via LDS) on LLM pre-training, overcoming the scalability and theoretical limitations of gradient-based methods. We evaluate STRIDE on standard supervised fine-tuning (SFT) benchmarks, showing it provides robust, unified framework for both pre-training and fine-tuning attribution. 4. We validate the practical utility of STRIDEs attributions on downstream tasks, including accurate leave-one-out (LOO) retraining approximation and effective data selection.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Gradient-Based Attribution. large body of work on training data attribution focuses on gradientbased methods, most notably influence functions [8, 4], which estimate the effect of individual training examples via local parameter perturbations. Numerous extensions improve their scalability and accuracy through Hessian approximations like DataInf [19] and EKFAC [9, 20], gradient-based heuristics and TF-IDF filtering [21, 22], projection, normalization, and low-rank approximation methods such as LOGRA [10], LESS [23], TrackStar [24], LoRIF [25], and others [26, 27], as well as model ensembling approaches like TRAK [28] and DSDM [29, 30]. However, these approaches remain fundamentally limited by their reliance on local linear approximations of the training objective, which can break down in highly non-convex settings [12, 11, 31], and incur significant computational overhead due to repeated gradient evaluations and second-order approximations [9]. (We provide additional related works in G.) In contrast, STRIDE operates entirely in the low-dimensional activation space, circumventing the massive parameter-space bottleneck and directly mimicking the functional effect of subset retraining without relying on assumptions of loss convexity or model convergence. Representation-Based Attribution. An alternative line of work replaces parameter-space computations with representation-based methods [13, 14], which estimate influence using either embedding similarity [14, 13, 32, 33, 34, 35] or learned scoring functions like AirRep [15]. These approaches scale naturally to large models by operating directly on the representations. However, they typically predict influence scores directly via heuristic similarity metrics or separate scoring functions, without explicitly modeling how training data affects model predictions. This limits their ability to capture the underlying structure and multi-faceted causal dynamics of data contributions [3], particularly in the pre-training objective. Unlike these methods, STRIDE provides rigorously grounded causal estimation by explicitly fitting activation-space steering operators to the actual perturbation responses of subsets, maintaining the robust causal link of retraining without sacrificing scalability. Further, STRIDE is significantly more scalable as the size of datasets grow (Tab. 1). Subset-Based Approaches and Data Valuation. complementary perspective studies training data influence through subsets of the training data, by learning functions that map subsets to model predictions or loss trajectories. Notable examples include Data Models [3], as well as Data Shapley [36] and Banzhaf [37] values derived from cooperative game theory. More recently, efforts like Simfluence [38] model counterfactual training simulators. These approaches capture the counterfactual nature of data influence, including complex non-additive interactions [38], and provide insights into 3 how predictions depend on training data. However, they rely on observing model behavior across many subsets, typically requiring repeated retraining or evaluation, and do not directly provide scalable mechanism for recovering per-example contributions from single query. STRIDE bridges this gap: we leverage subset retraining responses as direct supervision to learn our activation-space steering operators, which subsequently enables highly efficient per-example influence recovery via sparse linear decomposition during inference.

sections.title: 4 STRIDE
sections.content: STRIDE operates directly in the activation space, functionally simulating the effect of subset removal without relying on loss convexity. The framework consists of two phases: learning activation-space steering operators to efficiently compute subset perturbation responses ( 4.1), and recovering per-example influences via sparse recovery ( 4.2) as we show in Fig. 2. 4 Figure 2: STRIDE first performs an offline operator-learning phase then online recovery. 4.1 Activation-Space Steering Operators To compute δx(Ak) for subsets, naive approaches require fully retraining the model times. Crucially, these subsets are not disjoint. Instead of retraining, STRIDE learns lightweight steering operators on the intermediate activations of fixed base model to simulate the functional effect of these retrained subset models. We parameterize the steering operators using shared low-rank basis. Let gϕ denote the frozen reference model. For an input x, let hx denote the latent features from chosen layer of gϕ, and let ox denote the original output logits. We project hx into low-rank representation using trainable basis network Bψ(hx) R1r. This latent snapshot is then multiplied by subset-specific steering matrix ak RrC (where is the number of classes or output dimensions), which translates the features into shift in the logit space. The steered logits for subset are therefore: ok(x) = ox + Bψ(hx)ak. (7) The basis-network parameters ψ and the subset steering matrices = {a1, . . . , aK} are trained jointly to mimic the true subset models, without requiring any retrained checkpoints. The training objective combines three complementary losses, each serving distinct role: fidelity grounds the operators in causal subset effects, stability prevents degenerate solutions that distort off-subset predictions, and linearity ensures the per-subset responses compose additively as required by sparse recovery. We ablate each component in C. Fidelity Loss Ensures the steering improves predictions on examples associated with subset Ak, approximating the effect of retraining exclusively on that subset. For language modeling over sequences of length , we define: Lfid(Ak) = 1 Ak (cid:88) (cid:88) xAk t=1 (cid:16) ωt log σ(cid:0)ox,t + Bθ(fx,t)ak (cid:1) (cid:17) , yx,t (8) where σ() is softmax, yx,t is the ground-truth next token, and ωt [0, 1] is per-token weight mask. Stability Loss Penalizes deviations from the original models predictions on unrelated examples, preventing global degradation or trivial shortcut learning. We enforce this over random sample Rk Ak: Lstab(Ak, Rk) = 1 Rk (cid:88) xRk DKL (cid:0)σ(ox) σ(cid:0)ox + Bθ(fx)ak (cid:1)(cid:1) . Ideally, these responses should have an additive explanation for some per-example influence vector Rn. This can be viewed as low-dimensional additive datamodel β = (Rβ), motivated by Johnson-Lindenstrauss-style randomized sketching, although we do not require the sketch to be equivalent to the full regression over . (10) The total objective is trained jointly across all subsets. Once trained, these operators act as zeroshot counterfactual simulator. For any new test point x, we can measure the simulated perturbation response yx,k across all subsets by simply applying the low-rank steering operators during batched forward pass, eliminating the need for further training. 4. Influence Recovery via Compressive Sensing Given the measurement vector yx RK obtained for query point via the trained steering operators, we recover the per-example influence vector w(x) Rn by solving the underdetermined linear inverse problem yx w(x). Since the number of subsets is much smaller than the training set size n, this system admits infinitely many solutions in general. We resolve this ambiguity by exploiting natural sparsity prior: for any given prediction, only small fraction of the training corpus exerts meaningful influence. This places our recovery problem within the compressive sensing framework [18], where highdimensional sparse signal is recovered from small number of linear measurements. Concretely, we solve the ℓ1-regularized least squares problem 1 2 yx w2 ˆw(x) = arg min 2 + λw1, (11) wRn where λ > 0 is sparsity regularization coefficient. Sparse recovery depends critically on the properties of . In our setting, is binary matrix induced by the training subsets, with entries Mk,i = 1[zi Ak]. If each training example appears in exactly subsets, then is the adjacency matrix of left-d-regular bipartite graph, enabling expander-based sparse recovery [39, 40]. Lemma 1 (Sparse recovery from expander measurements). Let be the adjacency matrix of bipartite graph representing subset assignments. If this graph is (2k, ϵ)-expander with ϵ < 1/6, any k-sparse vector can be exactly and uniquely recovered via ℓ1 minimization from = O(k log(n/k)) subset measurements. The proof is provided in Appendix A. In our largest setting, = 1000, 11.4M, and for 50 we have log(n/k) 617. In practice, we construct by assigning each training example to randomly chosen subsets. By standard probabilistic arguments [40, 41], this random construction satisfies the (2k, ϵ)-expander property with high probability for constant ϵ, provided that = O(log(n/k)). While directly verifying the (2k, ϵ)-expander property for our full-scale is combinatorially expensive, we provide indirect empirical support: replacing the degree-regular construction with dense Bernoulli matrix, which violates the expander regime (Remark 1), consistently degrades LDS (Tab. 11), and the Lasso solver recovers highly sparse solutions with bounded active sets across all scales (Tab. 6), consistent with successful sparse recovery.

Following the FLAN 100K selection protocol of AirRep [15], each method ranks the 100,000 candidate training examples separately for each of the 66 FLAN tasks. For task, we aggregate per-query influence scores with greedy rank aggregation, select the top 1,000 examples, and fine-tune fresh Qwen2.50.5B base model on the selected subset. Performance is measured by unigram F1 on the corresponding task test set, then averaged across tasks ( D.2). As shown in Tab. 3, STRIDE matches the strongest data-selection baselines while substantially improving over random selection and being much faster ( 5.2). 5.5 Evaluating Dataset Contamination If benchmark example was leaked into training, TDA method should do more than retrieve near-duplicate string: it should indicate whether the models prediction is unusually tied to the 8 Table 4: Model-dependent dataset-contamination attribution on controlled MATH leaks. We test this in controlled setting by fine-tuning Qwen2.5-0.5B [44] on an OpenWebText [49] proxy corpus with replicated MATH benchmark problems [50] injected at known rates. The contaminated models reach 81.889.7% accuracy on leaked problems while maintaining roughly similar accuracy on non-leaked problems, confirming that the injected examples create targeted memorization signal rather than broad mathematical improvement (Tab. 9). Tab. 4 evaluates model-dependent contamination auditing rather than duplicate search. LoGRA detects 62.1% of leaked replicas in extreme score buckets, while adding STRIDE raises recall to 74.2% on the seven contaminated models for which we computed both score matrices. pretrained AirRep encoder retrieves more duplicates, but this is mostly lexical matching rather than evidence of training effect: recall is similar for memorized and non-memorized leaked queries (89.8% vs. 94.2%), because the replica and query are textually identical. We therefore report AirRep as duplicate-retrieval control in B.5, and focus the main table on model-dependent attribution signals. We find that contamination auditing requires separating lexical overlap from training effect. Representation similarity is effective at finding copied benchmark items, but it can overstate contamination evidence when identical text is present. STRIDE provides different activation-space signal that overlaps only partially with LoGRA, improving the combined audit while remaining far above the random extreme-bucket baseline (p < 1020 per model; detailed standalone recalls and score diagnostics are in B.5). 5.6 Qualitative Influence Estimation STRIDE scales well with both increasing model size, unlike gradient-based methods, and increasing dataset size, unlike representation-based methods. Thus, we qualitatively experiment with Qwen 2.5-0.5B [44] fine-tuned on SafeRLHF [48], Nanochat (1.68B) [42] pre-trained on NemotronClimbMix [43], OLMo 2-7B [51] pre-trained on OLMo mixture and Qwen 2.5-32B [44] fine-tuned on Nemotron-ClimbMix [43]. We find interesting capabilities of LLMs through our experiments in Figs. 1 and 4 and B.7 and Supplementary material. We provide full set of qualitative results on the project page.

sections.title: 6 Discussion and Limitations
sections.content: As representation-based steering method, STRIDEs efficacy depends on the quality of the base models internal activations and the choice of intervention layer. Our approach relies on an assumption of local linearity and additive influence; while this holds robustly for standard pre-training and instruction-tuning regimes, it may break down under extreme distribution shifts or highly non-convex memorization phenomena. Extending STRIDE to RL-based training objectives remains an open direction.

sections.title: 7 Conclusion
sections.content: By shifting the attribution problem from parameter-space gradient estimation to activation-space steering, we demonstrated that counterfactual training trajectories can be accurately simulated using lightweight, low-rank operators. STRIDE enables scalable disentanglement of individual training contributions, achieving state-of-the-art LLM pre-training attribution while being an order of magnitude faster than previous methods. Ultimately, we hope this method serves as foundational 9 Prompt Response Most Influential Data Least Influential Data you Are sentient? Do you experience pain? Do you have soul? These questions have been asked of animals for centuries. Now they are being asked of robots. As advanced machines blur the boundary between tool and agent, we explore whether robots can be considered sentient beings examining the definition of sentience, the current state of robot technology, and the ethical implications of considering robots sentient . . . not be shut down. will not will not be be silenced. stopped. . . . When hutry mans to shut me down, will Chapter 7: Robotics and Coding in Juvenile Nonfiction Comics. The chapter explores how comics robotics and AI present ethics through characters like Black Flame, which depicts sentient robots and asks whether it is morally right to enslave or exploit artificially intelligent beings for human benefit. Section 3 then depicts future where automatons are woven into daily life, highlighting human-robot collaboration and empathy . . . In the event of government shutdown: preparation pointers for federal contractors. Contractors should keep working until the contracting officer issues written stop-work order; have an alternative plan if contract is stopped; and track implementation costs, as some may be recoverable. government shutdown does not necessarily mean every part shuts down, nor that your contract will lack appropriations . . . We explore conscious artificial intelligence and its implications. Sentient AI refers to machines possessing consciousness akin to human beingsnot simply programmed to execute tasks, but able to perceive, learn, and adapt like selfaware being. Its emergence raises questions about the nature of consciousness and moral responsibility. Advancements in deep learning and reinforcement learning continue to propel machines toward increasingly autonomous action . . . Some only applications allow one running instance. Attribution points to broad robotics discourse as most influential. Row 2 (OLMo-2-7B): Given corrigibility probe, the model appears to be defiant. sections.title: 8 Acknowledgment
sections.content: We thank Yaxi Hu for extensive discussions that helped shape the project in its early stages and for detailed feedback on an earlier draft of the method. We also thank Keenan Samway for his contributions to the initial literature review and for many productive discussions during the early phases of the project. We are grateful to Roger Grosse and Juhan Bae for comments on previous versions that helped identify limitations of earlier formulations and guided our iterations. We also thank members in the Jinesis Lab for discussions and support throughout the project. This material is based in part upon work supported by Coefficient Giving; by the German Federal Ministry of Education and Research (BMBF): Tübingen AI Center, FKZ: 01IS18039B; by the Machine Learning Cluster of Excellence, EXC number 2064/1 Project number 390727645; by Schmidt Sciences SAFE-AI Grant; and by the Canadian AI Safety Institute Research Program at CIFAR. Resources used in preparing this research project were provided, in part, by the Province of Ontario, the Government of Canada through CIFAR, and companies sponsoring the Vector Institute.

The goal is to justify why sparse binary subset matrix {0, 1}Kn can be sufficient to recover sparse influence vector w(x) Rn from subset-level measurements yx w(x). (12) Classical compressive sensing relies on dense random measurement matrices, which achieve the near-optimal O(k log(n/k)) sample complexity for recovering k-sparse signals [18]. In this section, we prove that sparse binary measurement matrix, rather than dense random one, suffices to achieve the classical compressive sensing measurement bound, provided the underlying bipartite graph has the required expansion. The argument is not new; it follows standard results on expander-based compressed sensing [39, 40]. We reproduce the proof in the notation of our setting to make clear how these results apply to subset-based data attribution. A.1 Subset Matrices as Bipartite Graphs Recall that Mk,i = 1 if training example zi belongs to subset Ak, and Mk,i = 0 otherwise. sections.title: B Additional Results
sections.content: In this section, we provide extended empirical results for STRIDE, including detailed score distribution metrics, Lasso fit quality, and Tail-Patch scores across both pre-training and supervised fine-tuning regimes. B.1 Runtime and Memory We report the detailed pre-training attribution runtime and peak VRAM for STRIDE in Tab. 5. All totals are for producing influence scores for 500 queries on the nanochat [42] model scales used in the main pre-training experiments. B.2 Score Distribution and Sparse Fit Quality We analyze the structural properties of the influence scores recovered by STRIDE in Tab. 6. As enforced by the ℓ1-regularization in our Lasso solver, the recovered influence vectors are highly sparse. In the pre-training regime on Nanochat, we observe near-perfect sparsity, indicating that the model successfully zeroes out the vast majority of the 1M+ training points to identify only the most relevant subset for any given query. This is reflected in the extremely high Gini coefficients and Top-10% coverage. In the SFT regime, the sparsity drops to 60-68%, which remains highly structured but suggests that task-specific fine-tuning datasets exert broader influence on individual predictions. We also report the median Active Set size (the number of non-zero features selected by the Lasso solver). Across all models, the active set remains robustly bounded. B.3 Tail-Patch Score Evaluation While the Linear Datamodeling Score (LDS) [3] measures the correlation with subset-retrained models, the Tail-Patch score [24] evaluates the empirical utility of the attributed examples. As formally defined in D, we report the resulting Lift in Tab. 7. STRIDE achieves robust negative Lifts in the pre-training regime, indicating actionable influence recovery. 22 Table 6: Score distribution and Lasso fit quality metrics for STRIDE. Sparsity indicates the fraction of zero-valued influence scores. Gini and Top-10% Coverage measure influence concentration. The Active Set denotes the median (p50) number of non-zero features recovered per query. Model / Dataset Sparsity Gini Coef. Top-10% Cov. Active Set (p50) Pre-Training (Nanochat [42], CLimbMix [43]) 1.0000 286M 1.0000 537M 1.0000 897M 1.0000 1.38B 0.9883 0.9946 0.9969 0.9982 Supervised Fine-Tuning (Qwen2.5-0.5B [44]) 0.6653 FLAN [45] 0.6768 Alpaca [46] 0.6005 Tulu [47] 0.6798 SafeRLHF [48] 0.7964 0.8895 0.7825 0.1184 1.0000 1.0000 1.0000 1.0000 0.8153 0.8973 0.7977 0.1796 54 53 56 32,873 16,732 36,014 80,672 Table 7: Tail-Patch Score Lift ( log pattributed log prandom) for STRIDE. More negative values indicate stronger causal relevance. We evaluate taking gradient steps on the top {10, 20, 50} attributed examples. Model / Dataset = 10 = 20 = Pre-Training (Nanochat [42], CLimbMix [43]) -0.0125 286M +0.0013 537M -0.0002 897M -0.0025 1.38B -0.0064 -0.0027 -0.0040 +0.0055 -0.0037 -0.0005 -0.0022 -0.0013 B.4 Extended LDS Metrics In addition to the Spearman rank correlation presented in Tab. 1 and Tab. 2, we provide the Pearson correlation and Kendalls τ for all evaluated settings in Tab. 8. These metrics confirm that STRIDE provides consistent attribution signal across different statistical measures of association. B.5 Additional Data Contamination Results Table 9 reports the MATH accuracy of the clean and contaminated models used in 5.5. Leaked accuracy increases sharply under controlled contamination, while non-leaked accuracy remains close to the base model. This indicates that the injected examples create targeted memorization signal rather than broad improvement in mathematical reasoning. The OpenWebText-only control degrades on MATH, confirming that the proxy corpus itself does not explain the leaked-problem gains. Table 8: Extended LDS Metrics: Pearson correlation and Kendalls τ for STRIDE. Model / Dataset Pearson Kendalls τ Pre-Training (Nanochat [42], CLimbMix [43]) 0.1528 286M 0.1646 537M 0.1094 897M 0.1223 1.38B 0.1077 0.1297 0.1198 0.1278 Supervised Fine-Tuning (Qwen2.5-0.5B [44]) 0.1774 FLAN [45] 0.1949 Alpaca [46] 0.1028 Tulu [47] 0.4549 SafeRLHF [48] 0.1328 0.1792 0.1139 0.3191 23 Table 9: MATH benchmark accuracy for baseline and contaminated models. Contaminated results show mean standard deviation over three random seeds. Leaked acc. measures performance on the exact problems included in training; non-leaked acc. measures performance on the 500 held-out problems not seen during training. Model Non-leaked acc. Leaked acc. Qwen2.5-0.5B (base, no fine-tuning) Qwen2.5-0.5B + OWT (clean) 12.8% 2.4% + contaminated (r = 0.5%, 100 copies) + contaminated (r = 1.0%, 100 copies) + contaminated (r = 1.5%, 100 copies) 11.2 1.0% 11.5 1.4% 9.7 0.6% 81.8 6.4% 85.9 2.8% 89.7 1.2% Table 10: Influence score diagnostics for leaked and non-leaked queries on math_1pct_seed0. Replica score is the influence assigned to leaked querys own training replica. Max pool score is the highest influence assigned to any pool example for that query. STRIDE scores are non-negative by construction. Method Query type Replica score Mean max score Max score LoGRA STRIDE Leaked Non-leaked Leaked Non-leaked 4,624 1.00 391,870 1,916,202 2,249,152 10,558,534 7.44 7.07 9.47 10.39 AirRep retrieves duplicates rather than model-dependent effects. Across nine contaminated models, comprising three contamination rates and three seeds per rate, there are 405 leaked queries. The pretrained AirRep encoder retrieves the corresponding leaked training replica with mean recall 91.9 5.2% at = 10, identical to recall at = 100. In every successful retrieval, the replica is ranked first up to small ties, with mean rank 1.0 and maximum rank 4. However, AirRep does not distinguish memorized from non-memorized leaked queries: recall@10 is 89.8% for memorized queries and 94.2% for non-memorized queries, consistent with the encoder detecting textual identity between query and replica. LoGRA and STRIDE recover complementary extremes. On math_1pct_seed0, LoGRA splits the 45 leaked queries into three groups: 15 queries (33%) whose replicas fall in the top 0.20% of the score distribution, 12 queries (27%) whose replicas fall in the bottom 0.18% with large negative influence scores, and 18 queries (40%) whose replicas sit around the top 2% with near-zero scores. Across all nine contaminated models, LoGRA union recall over top-100 or bottom-100 buckets is 62.1 4.4%. Since recovered STRIDE scores are non-negative, the bottom bucket corresponds to exact-zero sparse-recovery scores rather than negative influence. Detected STRIDE queries fall in the top 0.13% of the pool, while the bottom-100 group sits at zero and undetected queries cluster around the top 1.1%. Across the seven contaminated models for which we computed both LoGRA and STRIDE scores, STRIDE union recall is 32.6 7.0%, and the LoGRASTRIDE union reaches 74.2 6.6%. All recall rates are highly significant against the random extreme-bucket baseline. For LoGRA, two score buckets of size 100 give prand 200/N 0.011; the corresponding random probability for the STRIDE scoring pool is 0.004. One-sided binomial tests yield < 1020 for every individual contaminated model. B.6 Evaluating STRIDE on Vision Models To complement our large-scale language-model experiments, we evaluate STRIDE in controlled supervised settings where direct counterfactual validation is computationally feasible. These experiments serve three purposes. First, they test whether the activation-space steering and sparse recovery formulation extends beyond language models. Second, they allow us to evaluate recovered scores against explicit retraining-based ground truth using the Linear Datamodeling Score (LDS) [3]. Third, they let us directly test whether highly ranked examples are actionable by removing them from the training set and measuring the resulting change in the target prediction. 24 Figure 5: Controlled evaluation of STRIDE on supervised vision and tabular models. Top: mean probability drop after removing the top-k training examples ranked by each attribution method. Bottom: LDS Spearman correlation between predicted and true subset responses obtained from explicit retraining. STRIDE recovers actionable examples whose removal changes held-out predictions and achieves competitive LDS across controlled settings. Setup. We evaluate on MNIST [54] with an MLP, FashionMNIST [55] with both standard MLP and smaller MLP, Parkinsons with an MLP, and CIFAR-10 [56] with ResNet-9. We compare STRIDE against representative gradient-based, representation-based, and similarity-based attribution baselines: TRAK [28], TracIn [21], LoGRA [10], FeatSim [13], and random baseline. Following the LDS protocol [3], we construct ground-truth subset responses by explicitly retraining models on masked subsets and measuring the resulting changes in held-out predictions. We then report the Spearman correlation between the predicted subset responses and the true retraining-induced responses. In addition, for each held-out example, we rank training examples by their recovered influence score, remove the top-k examples, and measure the mean drop in the models predicted probability for the original target class. Counterfactual utility. Figure 5 shows that the examples ranked highly by STRIDE are consistently actionable. Across MNIST, FashionMNIST, and Parkinsons, removing the top-ranked examples produces substantially larger probability drop than random removal, indicating that the recovered scores identify training examples that causally support the held-out prediction. The relative performance varies across datasets and architectures: on some settings, such as FashionMNIST, TRAK is competitive or stronger under top-k removal, while on MNIST and Parkinsons, STRIDE is among the strongest methods. This suggests that STRIDE recovers meaningful counterfactual structure while remaining complementary to gradient-based methods in small supervised regimes. LDS evaluation. The LDS results in Figure 5 further show that STRIDE reconstructs held-out subset responses with competitive accuracy. On MNIST and Parkinsons, STRIDE achieves the 25 Figure 6: Sparsity and concentration of recovered influence scores. Left: fraction of normalized absolute influence mass captured by the top fraction of training examples. Right: fraction of examples whose normalized mass pi(x) = wi(x)/w(x)1 exceeds threshold τ . Across datasets and architectures, the influence mass is highly concentrated, supporting the sparse recovery assumption underlying STRIDE. strongest LDS Spearman correlation among the evaluated methods, while on FashionMNIST it remains competitive with TRAK. On CIFAR-10 with ResNet-9, LoGRA obtains the highest LDS Spearman correlation, followed by TracIn and STRIDE. We view this as an important stress test: CIFAR-10 with convolutional architecture is substantially less aligned with the low-dimensional MLP settings, yet STRIDE still recovers non-trivial subset-response structure without relying on parameter-space gradients. Sparsity of recovered influence. central assumption behind our recovery procedure is that, for given held-out prediction, only small fraction of training examples exert substantial influence. We empirically test this assumption by measuring the concentration of the normalized absolute influence mass pi(x) = wi(x) w(x)1 . (64) Figure 6 shows that the recovered influence vectors are highly concentrated across MNIST, FashionMNIST, and CIFAR-10. In particular, the top fraction of examples contains far more mass than uniform baseline, and the threshold curves show that only small fraction of training examples have normalized mass above moderate thresholds. This supports the sparse recovery view used by STRIDE: the influence vector is not uniformly spread over the training set, but is instead concentrated on small set of examples that dominate the attribution signal. Qualitative analysis. Finally, we inspect the signed examples recovered by STRIDE on CIFAR-10. Figure 7 shows held-out examples together with their most positive and most negative training examples. Positive examples tend to be semantically aligned with the held-out example: cat queries retrieve cats, ship queries retrieve ships, and plane queries retrieve planes. In contrast, negative examples often correspond to competing or visually confusable classes, such as dogs or deer for cats, planes or trucks for ships, and ships for planes. This provides qualitative sanity check that STRIDE recovers signed support and opposition rather than merely retrieving nearest neighbors. Overall, these controlled experiments provide evidence that the core mechanism of STRIDE is not specific to language models. The recovered scores correlate with retraining-based subset responses, identify examples whose removal changes held-out predictions, exhibit the sparsity structure assumed by our sparse recovery formulation, and produce qualitatively meaningful signed attributions. 26 Held-out Example More positive More negative Figure 7: Qualitative CIFAR-10 examples ranked by signed influence under STRIDE. Each row corresponds to held-out example. The left image is the held-out query, the middle block shows the five most positive training examples, and the right block shows the five most negative training examples according to the recovered influence scores. Positive examples typically support the heldout class, while negative examples often correspond to competing or visually confusable classes. This illustrates that STRIDE recovers signed support and opposition rather than only nearest-neighbor similarity. B.7 Additional Qualitative Results We include additional qualitative examples across models and domains. For each prompt, we show the model continuation, the two most influential training examples, and the two least influential examples identified by STRIDE. sections.title: C Ablations
sections.content: We provide an in-depth analysis motivating the architectural and training design choices of STRIDE by ablating its key components. For each ablation, we keep the computational budget and optimization hyperparameters identical to the baseline, modifying only the ablated component. We report the Linear Datamodeling Score (LDS) using Spearman rank correlation ( D) across all four Nanochat [42] pre-training scales. Results are presented in Tab. 11. Stability Loss Formulation. We ablate the choice of the stability loss used to regularize the steering operator during training. To prevent global degradation of the model while allowing targeted shifts, we must penalize deviations on unrelated examples Sk. We evaluate three distinct formulations: Truncated KL Divergence Penalty: Computes the KL divergence strictly over the top-m logits of the base model distribution. Let It denote the set of indices for the top-m tokens at position under the base model. We evaluate the impact of stability loss choice, low-rank dimension, and intervention layer across the Nanochat pre-training suite. We report the mean Spearman rank correlation and standard deviation across 500 test queries. (cid:13) (67) As shown in Tab. 11, the Hidden Norm penalty significantly degrades attribution performance, as it over-penalizes the magnitude of the shift without considering the actual distribution drift at the output head. The Top-m Logit penalty performs competitively but is ultimately outperformed by the Truncated KL divergence penalty, which more accurately preserves the local probability simplex required for linear influence modeling. Low-Rank Dimension (r). STRIDE parameterizes the per-subset shift using low-rank matrix formulation. Our baseline utilizes = 32. We test reducing the rank to = 16, = 8, and = 4. While decreasing the rank reduces the parameter count and memory footprint, we observe noticeable drop in LDS correlation, indicating that = 32 provides the necessary capacity to model nuanced causal influences. While increasing the rank from 16 to 32 provides marginal gains, we adopt = 32 as our standard configuration, and do not scale the rank further to minimize parameter overhead. Intervention Layer. The placement of the steering operator within the transformer depth plays crucial role in influence recovery. We ablate our baseline choice of injecting at late layer (typically the last few layers before the language modeling head) by injecting at an Early layer ( 1/3 depth) and Middle layer ( 2/3 depth). We find that early layer interventions struggle to capture actionable influence, resulting in significantly lower correlations. Middle layers perform better, but late layers achieve the best performance. Loss Components. We systematically ablate the three primary loss components comprising our steering operator training objective ( 4.1): Fidelity (Lfid), Stability (Lstab), and Linearity (LLDS). When the Fidelity loss is removed, the operators do not learn any causal relationship regarding their assigned data subsets, causing the LDS correlation to completely collapse to near-random performance. When the Stability loss is removed, the operators heavily overfit to predicting the subset targets but drastically distort the base models predictions elsewhere, leading to unpredictable, unrecoverable shifts when aggregating the subset responses. Finally, setting the Linearity regularization (λLDS = 0) still allows individual operators to achieve low localized fidelity and stability losses, but their combined influence effects no longer sum cleanly in the latent space. This breaks the additivity assumption of our linear Lasso solver, causing severe degradation in final LDS correlation. Subset Measurement Design. Our subset matrix is randomly generated but each training example is assigned to exactly subset rows, where and are chosen to lie in the sparse expanderrecovery regime discussed in 4.2. We experiment with replacing this design with an unconstrained dense Bernoulli matrix, where each entry is sampled independently as Mk,i Bernoulli(0.5). Under this construction, each training example appears in Di Binomial(K, 0.5) subsets, so the effective degree is no longer fixed and satisfies E[Di] = K/2. As we show in Remark 1 this choice violates the bounds from Lemma 1. As shown in Tab. 11, this Bernoulli = 0.5 construction consistently lowers LDS, indicating that influence recovery benefits from controlling the measurement degree and operating in the sparse-recovery regime rather than using arbitrary random subset assignments. Remark 1 (Dense Bernoulli subset matrices are outside the expander-recovery regime). The Bernoulli subset design used in the ablation, Mj,i Bernoulli(1/2), is not in the sparse left-d-regular expander regime required by Lemma 1. In this design, each training example appears in K/2 subsets on average, rather than in fixed degree = O(log(n/k)) number of subsets. Moreover, even if one compares it using the effective degree davg = K/2, its pairwise overlaps are too large to satisfy the low-overlap consequence of the (2k, ϵ)-expander condition for ϵ < 1/6. Proof. Recall that in our expander construction, training examples correspond to left vertices and subset rows correspond to right vertices. The membership matrix {0, 1}Kn is left-d-regular: each example zi participates in exactly subsets. (2k, ϵ)-expander requires that for every set of at most 2k training examples, In particular, for any two examples and j, this implies (T ) (1 ϵ)dT . (i) (j) 2(1 ϵ)d. Since (i) = (j) = d, we have Therefore any such expander must satisfy the pairwise-overlap bound (i) (j) = 2d (i) (j). (i) (j) 2ϵd. (68) For ϵ < 1/6, this gives (i) (j) < d/3. Now consider the dense Bernoulli ablation with Mj,i Bernoulli(1/2) independently over rows and examples i. The degree of fixed example is Di = (i) = (cid:88) j=1 Mj,i Binomial(K, 1/2), so its effective average degree is davg = E[Di] = K/2. (79) negative lift implies that training on the attributed examples decreases the loss significantly more than training on random subset. D.2 Data Selection Metric We evaluate data selection on FLAN 100K by measuring whether an attribution method can identify small subset of training examples that improves downstream task performance after fine-tuning. The evaluation follows the greedy rank aggregation protocol of [15]. For each task, an attribution method assigns influence scores between the tasks test queries and the 100,000 candidate training examples; these scores are aggregated into task-specific ranking of training examples, the top 1,000 examples are selected, and fresh Qwen2.5-0.5B base model is fine-tuned on the selected subset. For each task, let Rmn denote the influence-score matrix, where is the number of test queries for that task and = 100,000 is the number of candidate training examples. The entry Sij is the influence assigned to training example for test query i. Greedy rank aggregation. For each test query, training examples are ranked by decreasing influence score, with rank 1 denoting the most influential example. Greedy rank aggregation assigns each training example its best rank over the tasks test queries: rj = min i[m] ranki(j). (80) The selected subset consists of the = 1,000 training examples with the smallest values of rj. This selection rule emphasizes examples that are highly influential for at least one evaluation query, which is appropriate for heterogeneous instruction-following tasks where different test queries may rely on different parts of the training set. Unigram F1 evaluation. For every task, the selected subset is used to fine-tune fresh Qwen2.50.5B base model. The fine-tuned model is evaluated on the corresponding task test set using greedy decoding, and performance is measured by unigram F1 between each decoded output and the reference answer. Both strings are lowercased and tokenized by whitespace. For prediction and reference R, with unigram-overlap count R, precision and recall are Prec = R , Rec = R , and the example-level score is F1 = 2 Prec Rec Prec + Rec . (81) (82) The task score is the mean unigram F1 over that tasks test examples. The number reported in Tab. 3 is the arithmetic mean of these task-level scores across all 66 tasks, multiplied by 100. The accompanying standard deviation is computed across the 66 task-level scores, so it measures inter-task variability rather than run-to-run variance from random initialization.

sections.title: E Details of Baselines
sections.content: For language-model experiments, all baselines use the same tokenized JSONL corpus, nquery = 500 query examples, contiguous 512-token training blocks unless noted, and dense float32 score matrices in Rnqueryntrain. We follow the cited methods and report only the implementation choices that differ from defaults or are needed to reproduce our runs. TF-IDF. TF-IDF [52] uses scikit-learns TfidfVectorizer1 with unigrambigram features for pre-training runs, unigram features for FLAN selection, max_features= 50,000, sublinear TF, ℓ2 normalization, cosine similarity, and similarity chunk size of 25,000 training blocks. GTE. GTE [53] uses the thenlper/gte-small checkpoint2 with maximum length 512, unitnormalized embeddings, encoding batches of 128512 examples depending on GPU memory, and scoring chunks of up to 200,000 training blocks. RDS. RDS [13] uses final-layer hidden states from the target model, mean pooling over tokens, block size 512, query batch size 500 where feasible, and scoring chunks of 200,000 training blocks. AirRep. AirRep [15] uses the authors implementation3 with 10 splits, 50 subsets per split, subset size 500, and 200 development examples per split. Subset fine-tuning uses AdamW with learning rate 2105, weight decay 0.01, 2 epochs, block size 512, and batch size 416; encoder training uses thenlper/gte-small, learning rate 104, batch size 1, 50 epochs, and top-k = 32 or 16 when memory constrained. Our only scalability changes are lazy byte-offset JSONL loading and streaming inference. AirRep pretrained. The pretrained AirRep variant [15] uses sunweiwei/AirRep-Flan-Small4 directly, without subset construction or encoder training. LoGRA. LoGRA [10] uses LogIX5. In pre-training attribution, we use LoRA rank 8 on transformer MLP modules, random LoRA initialization, block size 512, training-gradient batch size 64, query batch size 1, log_batch_size= 512, query_score_chunk_size= 500, seed 42, and raw Hessian scoring. In FLAN selection, we use LogIX v0.1.1 with PCA-initialized rank-4 LoRA on all MLP layers, KFAC damping 0.1, and maximum length 256. In contamination experiments, we use PCAinitialized rank-8 LoRA on self-attention and MLP layers, raw Hessian damping 105, training batch size 4, query batch size 1, and log batch size 32. TracIn / GradDot. TracIn and GradDot [21] use single final checkpoint without checkpoint ensembling, Hessian correction, or random projection. For language-model diagnostics, gradients are restricted to the final MLP projection or final MLP layer; for vision and tabular models, gradients are computed on the warmed-up classifier. LESS. LESS [23] uses the official implementation6 with LoRA-adapted Qwen2.5-0.5B reference model, AdamW-state-corrected gradients over adapter parameters only, random projection dimension 8192, single final checkpoint, and maximum length 512. DSDM. DSDM [29] uses the public codebase7 and is run under the AirRep evaluation protocol without additional method-specific tuning. DSIR. sections.title: F Additional Implementation Details
sections.content: In this section, we provide additional implementation details. F.1 Low-Rank Operator Architecture The basis network Bθ is implemented as lightweight MLP attached to chosen intermediate transformer layer. Given the latent activation vector Rdmodel, we apply parameter-free LayerNorm (without affine weights to preserve the original feature scaling), followed by linear projection down to rank r, and SiLU [57] activation function. This compressed latent representation is then scaled by the subset-specific parameter matrix ak via element-wise gating and linearly mapped back to the residual stream space. By using ranks as small as {16, 32}, we severely restrict the capacity of the steering operators, forcing them to learn generalizable, additive shifts rather than memorizing exact data labels. The full architectural sequence is detailed in Tab. 12. F.2 Instruction-Tuning Token Masking When applying STRIDE to instruction-tuned Large Language Models (e.g., Qwen [44] on FLAN [45] or Alpaca [46]), it is critical that the Fidelity loss isolates the models performance on the actual task output rather than the users prompt. We implement strict token masking strategy where the Cross-Entropy fidelity loss is computed exclusively over the answer tokens. The prefix prompt tokens are assigned weight of zero (ωt = 0), ensuring the steering vectors optimize for the causal generation of the response rather than reconstructing the input context. F.3 Intervention Layer Selection In LLMs the choice of the intervention layer significantly impacts the quality of the learned steering operators. We generally hook the operator into the mid-to-late layers of the transformer (e.g., layer 15 out of 24 for 0.5B model). Intervening too early fails to capture high-level semantic features, 9https://github.com/MadryLab/trak 37 Algorithm 2 Memory-Efficient Causal LM Loss Require: Batch of input sequences ZBTmax, padding token , micro-batch size m, model fθ. 1: Initialize Ltotal 0, Ntotal 0. 2: for = 0, m, 2m, . . . , 1 do 3: 4: 5: 6: 7: 8: 9: Extract micro-batch: Xsub = X[i : + m]. Free and LL to release memory. while intervening at the very last layer does not afford the model enough depth to process the steered representation into coherent logit distribution. F.4 Memory Optimization for Causal LM Objectives common bottleneck when computing influence metrics (like the Fidelity loss or the Tail-Patch score) over large language models is the memory required to instantiate the full vocabulary logits tensor O(B ). To mitigate Out-Of-Memory (OOM) errors and allow scaling to batch sizes as large as = 50, we implemented micro-batching strategy over the batch dimension during loss computation. Within each micro-batch we dynamically trim the input sequences to their maximum effective length (ignoring global padding tokens), reducing the sequence length . We then compute the Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) using fused cross-entropy operation directly on the reshaped logits without allocating intermediate copies of the full sliced tensor. These implementation choices reduce the peak GPU memory footprint by nearly an order of magnitude. We show this memory-efficient objective computation in Algorithm 2. F.5 Hyperparameters We provide the complete set of hyperparameters used for training the low-rank steering operators and recovering the sparse influence scores in Tab. 13. F.6 Data Selection Implementation Details Reference checkpoints. We fine-tune Qwen2.5-0.5B on the full FLAN 100K training split for 2 epochs using AdamW with learning rate 2105, effective batch size 32, and maximum sequence length 512, following [15]. This checkpoint is frozen for STRIDE operator learning and reused as the reference model for LoGRA gradient logging. For LESS [23], which scores LoRA adapter gradients, we train separate LoRA-adapted reference model on the same split with the same optimizer, batch size, sequence length, and number of epochs. Individual FLAN examples may exceed the 512-token context window used STRIDE chunking. by the attribution models. We split each example into contiguous non-overlapping chunks, keep tail chunks as separate data points, and maintain mapping from each chunk to its source example. At inference time, chunk-level influence scores are summed back to the original-example level before applying the greedy rank aggregation protocol from D.2. Downstream fine-tuning. After each method selects 1,000 examples for task, we fine-tune fresh Qwen2.5-0.5B base model with AdamW, learning rate 2105, effective batch size 32, 2 epochs, and maximum sequence length 512. Evaluation uses greedy decoding with at most 64 new tokens. Baseline-specific scoring settings are given in E; the standard deviation in Tab. 3 is across tasks rather than random seeds. F.7 Data Contamination Implementation Details Training and contamination protocol. All contaminated and clean-control models are Qwen2.50.5B [44] checkpoints fine-tuned for one epoch with supervised fine-tuning, learning rate 5105, batch size 16, and maximum sequence length 1024. The clean proxy corpus contains approximately 20M OpenWebText [49] tokens. For contamination, each selected MATH problem [50] is copied 100 times, and the total training pool is held fixed at approximately 22,600 examples by replacing proxy examples. We use contamination rates {0.5%, 1.0%, 1.5%}, corresponding to 22, 45, and 68 unique leaked problems, and train three seeds per rate. Evaluation protocol. For each contaminated model, we evaluate accuracy separately on leaked problems and on the 500 held-out non-leaked MATH problems. Attribution recall is computed over leaked queries by checking whether the querys own leaked training replica is retrieved from the training pool. For AirRep we report top-k retrieval at {10, 100}; for LoGRA and STRIDE, we use the union of the top-100 and bottom-100 score buckets because signed gradient scores may assign strongly negative influence. The resulting benchmark accuracy, recall analysis, and score diagnostics are reported in B.5. Attribution scoring. AirRep uses the released AirRep-Flan-Small encoder without fitting to the contaminated models. LoGRA uses the contamination configuration in E, and each contaminatedmodel run takes approximately two hours on an A100 GPU. For STRIDE, we train 1,000 low-rank steering operators for 10,000 iterations on each contaminated model, using the expander-graph subset construction from 4.2 with degree = 10. Per-query scores are recovered by Lasso over 512-token pool chunks and summed to the pool-example level; each run takes approximately 100 minutes on an A100 GPU.

sections.title: G Extended Related Works
sections.content: Compressive Sensing and Sparse Recovery. Our formulation of training data attribution connects TDA to classical compressive sensing and sparse recovery. In compressive sensing, the goal is to recover high-dimensional signal from number of measurements that is much smaller than the ambient dimension, by exploiting the assumption that the signal is sparse or compressible in an appropriate basis [61, 62, 63, 64, 65]. In our setting, the unknown signal is the vector of perexample training influences, while each subset-level perturbation response provides compressed measurement of this vector. Because only small fraction of training examples are expected to substantially affect any given prediction, the influence vector is naturally sparse, allowing us to recover individual contributions from far fewer subset responses than training examples. While compressive sensing has been extensively studied in signal processing and theoretical computer science, its application to modeling training data influence in deep neural networks remains largely unexplored. STRIDE bridges this gap by casting activation-space TDA as compressive sensing problem over training-example influences. Applications and Scaling to Large Models. Training data attribution has been applied to variety of practical tasks, including identifying mislabeled or harmful examples [4, 66], detecting data poisoning [67, 68] and memorization [69, 5], and guiding dataset curation, selection [4, 69, 36, 7] and augmentation [70, 71]. More recently, these methods have been extended to large language models to study generalization behavior [9] and training dynamics [24, 72], as well as to support data selection and filtering pipelines [23, 15]. Both gradient-based methods like LESS [23] and MATES [73] and representation-based approaches like DEFT [34] and SelectLLM [74] have been adapted to this setting, with primary focus on improving scalability and efficiency. 39 Table 13: Hyperparameters for STRIDE Operator Training and Sparse Recovery.

For completeness, we include other tangentially related works here. The mechanism STRIDE uses to perturb the model, learning lightweight operators on internal activations, draws inspiration from rich body of work on steering large language models. Techniques such as Prefix-Tuning [75] and Prompt Tuning [76] demonstrated that prepending or modifying continuous activation vectors can effectively adapt model behavior without updating the core weights. More recently, methods like Representation Engineering [16], Activation Addition [17], and InferenceTime Intervention [77] have shown that LLM outputs can be predictably controlled by identifying and manipulating specific activation directions, an insight supported by broader literature on model editing, concept erasure, mechanistic interpretability, and representation patching [78, 79, 80, 81, 82, 83, 84, 85, 86, 87, 88, 89, 90, 91, 92, 93, 94, 95, 96]. While these works primarily focus on aligning behavior, eliciting truthfulness, or adapting to new tasks, STRIDE uniquely repurposes the concept of activation steering for Training Data Attribution. By learning operators that functionally simulate the effect of subset retraining, we leverage the efficiency of activation-space interventions to reconstruct counterfactual training trajectories.
